title_en: Hadith of Salah — The Prayer Rulings
content_en:
The Book of Salah in Umdah al-Ahkam contains the largest collection of hadiths in the text, reflecting the centrality of the five daily prayers in Islamic practice and the vast number of prophetic narrations that address their conditions, pillars, and recommended acts. The hadiths are drawn from Bukhari and Muslim and cover every major aspect of the prayer from the call to prayer through the closing salutation.

Among the most important hadiths in this section is the narration of the Misab ibn Sa'd from his father, in which the Prophet, peace be upon him, was asked which deed is most beloved to Allah, and he answered: 'Prayer in its time.' This hadith establishes the priority of performing prayers at their prescribed times above other righteous deeds, a ruling of profound practical importance for Muslim life. The subsequent hadiths on the prayer times specify the astronomical markers for each prayer — the whitening of the eastern horizon for fajr, the passage of noon for dhuhr, the shadow equaling the object for asr — grounding the daily schedule of worship in the physical world.

The hadith of Abu Humaid al-Sa'idi describing the Prophet's prayer is among the most important in this section, providing a detailed description of the physical acts of prayer including the raising of hands, the posture in ruku', the method of prostration, and the sitting between prostrations. This hadith serves as a comprehensive reference for the correct physical form of the prayer. Similarly, the hadiths of the man who prayed incorrectly (the 'man who prayed badly') record the Prophet's instruction: 'Return and pray, for you have not prayed,' followed by the minimum description of what constitutes a valid prayer.

The section also includes hadiths on congregational prayer, the virtue of the first row, the right of the imam, the way the follower should track the imam's movements, and the ruling for the latecomer. The hadiths on the Friday prayer, the two Eid prayers, and the prayer for the deceased (salat al-janazah) are included, providing a complete hadith foundation for the full range of Salah rulings.
